coredata.dc:title: Neem leaf extract as a sustainable green inhibitor for corrosion inhibition in RCC and strength improvement in concrete, in conjunction with spectroscopic characterization and statistical evaluation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Junaid Ahmed, E.S.
Mohan Ganesh, G.
coredata.dc:description: The demand for sustainable and environmentally benign green corrosion inhibitors (GCIs) for reinforced cement concrete (RCC) has increased due to the persistent occurrence of corrosion in concrete structures constructed in marine environments. In this study, the influence of neem extract on the strength enhancement of concrete was evaluated through mechanical testing, while its corrosion inhibition performance was assessed in reinforced cement concrete (RCC) samples exposed to 1M NaCl solution. This RCC based corrosion evaluation using neem leaf extract has not been previously reported. Compressive strength and electrochemical tests (Tafel, LPR, and EIS) were carried out for control, 11 ml and 29 ml neem extract containing samples. The 29 ml neem extract containing samples showed higher average strength than the control specimens, validated via SEM analysis which exhibited better CSH gel growth. From Tafel and LPR, the least corrosion rate (0.3759 mm/year) and highest polarization resistance (1647.9 Ω) was attained for the highest inhibitor concentration. The EIS outcome for all samples displayed Warburg impedance, along with a decrease in the double-layer capacitance with increasing neem concentration. The energy parameter values of phytochemical compounds in neem obtained via DFT showed strong concurrence with the statistical output of the principal component analysis (PCA). The significant compounds present in neem extract were detected using GC-MS. With neem leaves being widely available with less demand throughout the year, non-toxic, eco and environment-friendly, it can be used as a partial replacement in concrete to achieve better strength and durability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Neem extract
RCC
Compressive strength
EIS
SEM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental overflow of RCC specimens – casting, curing, exposure and testing.
Tafel plots of RCC samples immersed in 1M NaCl in the absence and presence of Neem extract.
LPR plots of RCC samples without and with different inhibitor concentrations.
a) Complex Plane plots of RCC samples without and with different inhibitor concentrations b) Circuit Fitting.
Bode modulus of impedance plot for the samples immersed in 1M NaCl solution.
EHOMO, ELUMO and ΔE values obtained from density functional theory [20].
SEM images of (a) control (b) 11 ml (c) 29 ml.

row.entry: RCC (70 mm × 100 mm concrete with 8 mm diameter TMT steel)

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This article is part of a special issue entitled: ASCTF published in Results in Surfaces and Interfaces.

head.ce:title: Neem leaf extract as a sustainable green inhibitor for corrosion inhibition in RCC and strength improvement in concrete, in conjunction with spectroscopic characterization and statistical evaluation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Structural and Geotechnical Engineering, School of Civil Engineering, Vellore Institute of Technology (VIT), Vellore, 632014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Structural and Geotechnical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Structural and Geotechnical Engineering, School of Civil Engineering, Vellore Institute of Technology (VIT), Vellore, 632014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The demand for sustainable and environmentally benign green corrosion inhibitors (GCIs) for reinforced cement concrete (RCC) has increased due to the persistent occurrence of corrosion in concrete structures constructed in marine environments. In this study, the influence of neem extract on the strength enhancement of concrete was evaluated through mechanical testing, while its corrosion inhibition performance was assessed in reinforced cement concrete (RCC) samples exposed to 1M NaCl solution. This RCC based corrosion evaluation using neem leaf extract has not been previously reported. Compressive strength and electrochemical tests (Tafel, LPR, and EIS) were carried out for control, 11 ml and 29 ml neem extract containing samples. The 29 ml neem extract containing samples showed higher average strength than the control specimens, validated via SEM analysis which exhibited better CSH gel growth. From Tafel and LPR, the least corrosion rate (0.3759 mm/year) and highest polarization resistance (1647.9 Ω) was attained for the highest inhibitor concentration. The EIS outcome for all samples displayed Warburg impedance, along with a decrease in the double-layer capacitance with increasing neem concentration. The energy parameter values of phytochemical compounds in neem obtained via DFT showed strong concurrence with the statistical output of the principal component analysis (PCA). The significant compounds present in neem extract were detected using GC-MS. With neem leaves being widely available with less demand throughout the year, non-toxic, eco and environment-friendly, it can be used as a partial replacement in concrete to achieve better strength and durability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reinforced cement concrete (RCC) is used worldwide for constructing diverse range of structures, such as bridges, culverts, buildings, dams, tunnels, roads, coastal protection works, onshore and off shore structures, wastewater treatment plants etc, due to its ability to strike a balance between performance and affordability. It provides high strength, versatility, and structural reliability. Nevertheless, these structures encounter serious corrosion complications when exposed to NaCl [1,2]. The repercussions often include significant financial losses, increased health and safety hazards, loss of life and time, and environmental pollution. Based on practical performance, TMT rebars outperformed other steels in the construction of RCC structures purely in terms of cost-effectiveness, superior strength, ductility and enhanced bonding with concrete [3].

Concrete with embedded steel rebars naturally maintains a higher pH range of 12.5 -13.5 primarily due to the presence of alkalis such as Na2O and K2O in cement. When water is introduced, these alkalis react to generate OH− ions, contributing to the elevated pH level, thus protecting the rebars to some extent [4]. The calcium hydroxide (Ca (OH)2) in cured concrete also plays a role in increasing the alkalinity but diminishes when the structures are exposed to corrosive species in the environment.

The deterioration of structures constructed in and around marine regions is primarily attributed to chloride ions. The rate and severity rely on the strength and quality of the concrete including its pore structure and crack characteristics etc. The passivation layer breaks down once the chloride ion concentration reaches the chloride threshold level (CTL), thereby initiating corrosion (metal loss and rust formation) [1]. Over time, the volume of rust increases and exceeds the volume of steel (2– 6 times), causing the concrete to fail, indirectly by cracking, spalling, and delamination [5]. In due course, this leads to loss of ductility in steel and reduction in the bond strength between steel and concrete.

One of the most effective practices for mitigating corrosion is the addition of inhibitors during the mixing stage of concrete. It is compatible with conventional concrete and exhibits uniform distribution within the mixes. Moreover, it also saves time, reduces labour and maintenance costs by prolonging the service life of structures, making it highly recommended for complex structures or congested reinforcements. Compared with organic corrosion inhibitors (synthetic, natural, and hybrid), inorganic inhibitors are more effective; however, their usage has slowed or discontinued in many countries due to environmental and health issues [6]. Over the years, research efforts have increasingly shifted towards the use of natural corrosion inhibitors, as they are economical, environmentally friendly, and biodegradable. Researchers have used different plant derived materials as green corrosion inhibitors (GCI) to impede corrosion in RCC [2,7–9]. Electrochemical corrosion techniques, comprising EIS and Tafel polarization, were utilised to examine the corrosion inhibition performance of RCC in NaCl solution incorporating methanolic extracts of Ricinus communis fruit residue and Prosopis juliflora leaf extract at concentrations of 25, 50 and 100 ppm, in separate studies conducted by the same research team. Among these, the 100 ppm concentration showed the lowest corrosion rate and double-layer capacitance values [2,10]. In another investigation, a single concentration (5%) of silver nanoparticles doped with Elaeis guineensis (EG) leaf extract was used as GCI to reduce the corrosion rate of reinforced cement concrete structures subjected to sea water, as analysed using LPR and Tafel polarization [8]. Furthermore, the combined role of mineral admixtures (metakaolin and micro silica) and aqueous Disporosis pernyi leaves extract (10, 50 and 100 mg/L) on the corrosion behaviour of RCC samples exposed to a NaCl medium was investigated using Tafel polarization and EIS procedures. The optimum concentration identified in this work was 100 mg/L, which displayed the lowest corrosion current density of 0.011 μA/cm2 and the lowest double-layer capacitance of steel of 0.6 μF/cm2 [9].

Limited research has been conducted in recent years to mitigate corrosion in RCC structures using green corrosion inhibitors, which include Licorice roots [11], orange peels [12], garlic peel charcoal [13], guar gum [14], gum Arabic nanoparticles [15], maize gluten meal [16], olive leaf [17] and pomegranate peel [18] extracts. Apart from these studies, no other significant investigations have been reported in recent years in using green corrosion inhibitors for RCC corrosion inhibition. Licorice roots were used as a GCI for the corrosion prevention of carbon steel rebars embedded in mortar specimens. Electrochemical investigations via EIS and Tafel measurements revealed that a 50% licorice and 50% sodium nitrite combination significantly diminished the corrosion rate in mortars containing 0.8% chlorides. The orange peel extract (3 wt%) was incorporated into the mixing water as an admixture during concrete preparation to evaluate the electrochemical behaviour of RCC samples. The findings revealed that the combination of 3 wt% orange peel extract and 2.4 wt% commercial admixture showed the best inhibition performance. A study employing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and Potentiodynamic polarization was conducted by adding guar gum (0.5%, 1.0%, 1.4%, and 1.5%) into cement during concrete mixing. The lowest corrosion current density of 302 μA/cm2 and double layer capacitance of 193 μF/cm2 were obtained for the samples admixed with 1.4% gum. The purpose of selecting neem (Azadirachta indica) leaf, as a plant-based GCI for RCC corrosion inhibition, is that it remains unexplored [19].

The core objective of this study is to mitigate corrosion in RCC by incorporating neem leaf extract at two different concentrations. The corrosion inhibiting performance is assessed using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), linear polarization resistance (LPR) and Tafel polarization techniques. The corrosion inhibition of RCC using neem leaf extract has not been reported previously. In addition, compressive strength testing was performed to evaluate the effectiveness of the concrete specimens and the results were further validated using SEM. The central importance of this work lies in mitigating corrosion in onshore and offshore structures constructed using reinforced cement concrete, particularly in splash zones (repeated wet-dry cycles of seawater). It is an incessant and serious durability concern encountered throughout the lifespan of such structures, predominantly due to presence of chloride ions in seawater. Although numerous corrosion mitigation techniques, including cathodic protection, coatings, corrosion-resistant alloys and synthetic organic inhibitors, have been repeatedly adopted in the past, their prolonged performance has frequently been curtailed due to issues such as mechanical damage, high initial and maintenance costs, design complexity, toxicity to marine and human life and poor biodegradability. Therefore, researchers have increasingly shifted their focus towards the use of green corrosion inhibitors to hinder corrosion in RCC, an area that has received limited investigation. Therefore, this study reports a novel application of using neem leaf extract as a green corrosion inhibitor to reduce corrosion rate in RCC, which has not been explored before. It represents an advancement in the field of corrosion inhibitors through the use of eco and environmentally benign neem extract, which also finds established applications in various fields (chiefly medicine). Neem leaves remain abundantly available despite their high demand in various other applications. The electrochemical corrosion behaviour of RCC incorporating neem leaf extract has not been examined beforehand. Additionally, statistical PCA analysis validated using DFT has not been documented elsewhere.

Leaves were collected from twigs in the Vellore region, carefully cleaned to remove surface impurities, shade-dried, powdered, and mixed with distilled water in a 1:10 ratio; subsequently, the mixture was left undisturbed for 24 h under aluminium foil cover. After 24 h, the mixture was filtered through Whatman filter paper to obtain a phytochemical-rich extract solution, and the solid residue was concurrently removed. The extraction of phytochemicals transpires through osmosis and diffusion within the solution during the soaking process. To meet the demands of capital-intensive infrastructure projects, maceration was adopted as the extraction method. The extraction was conducted at room temperature, since the majority of bioactive compounds found in neem are thermolabile and may undergo degradation at elevated temperatures. Further merits of this extraction approach include no necessity for sophisticated equipment or high-end technology, considerable scalability, low energy consumption and cost effectiveness. Water was used as the extraction solvent because it is economical, readily available in bulk, environmentally benign and well-suited to the cement hydration process.

Ordinary Portland cement (OPC) of 53 grade, M-sand passing through a 4.75 mm size and retained on a 150-μm sieve, angular coarse aggregate passing through a 10 mm sieve and retained on a 6.3 mm sieve, potable water, high-range water-reducing superplasticizer (Sika ViscoCrete), and TMT Fe550D steel rebars of 8 mm diameter were used. The mix proportions are presented in Table 1 . RCC specimens with and without neem extract, measuring 70 mm in diameter and 100 mm in height, were cast with centrally placed steel reinforcement, having a clear cover of 31 mm. The exposed steel area was maintained at 1 cm2. The specimens were cured for 28 days and subsequently subjected to alternate wet and dry cycles to simulate natural seawater conditions. The 1M NaCl solution was filled up to a concrete height of 90 mm to avoid direct contact of the solution with the protruding steel. The casting, curing, wet-dry cycles and electrochemical test setups are illustrated in Fig. 1 . Structures such as piers, jetties, seawalls, breakwaters, bridges (piles and abutments), docks, coastal buildings, RCC pavements near coastlines, lighthouse foundations, ports, offshore oil and gas structures, desalination plants, renewable energy structures are substantially affected by salt exposure.

Neem extract was incorporated as a partial replacement for water at concentrations of 11 ml and 29 ml per 500 ml of mixing water. These concentrations were chosen based on previous steel corrosion inhibition study carried out in the presence of neem extract [20]. In this work, the enhancement of mechanical strength and electrochemical properties was primarily examined at lower concentrations. The rationale for adopting these concentrations is discussed in detail in the discussion section below.

Compressive strength tests were performed on cube samples of dimensions 100 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm, at a loading rate of 2.3 kN/s. Specimens cured for 7 and 28 days were tested using a compression testing machine with a capacity of 3000 KN, in accordance with IS: 516 guidelines [21]. The average compressive strength of three specimens for each concentration is reported in the results section.

The tests were performed using a Vionic instrument manufactured by Metrohm. The experiments were carried out using Intello software and the data analysis was performed using Nova 2.1. A three-electrode system comprising a working electrode (test sample), a reference electrode (saturated calomel electrode), and a counter electrode (nichrome mesh) was immersed in the exposure solution prior to the start of the experiment. After completion of the wet and dry cycles, the open circuit potential (OCP) was first measured for the samples immersed in 1M NaCl, both in the presence and absence of neem extract, to establish a stable potential. Subsequently, non-destructive EIS was carried out over a frequency range of 100 kHz to 10 mHz using a sinusoidal input potential perturbation of 0.01 V. The impedance response of the samples was attained over the selected frequency range, and the results were analysed by interpreting the Nyquist plots through equivalent circuit fittings. A destructive polarization test was performed by applying a potential perturbation of ±200 mV at a scan rate of 1 mV/s with respect to the OCP. From this test, the corrosion current density and corrosion rate were directly obtained. The corrosion current density was calculated from the intersection of tangents (anode and cathode curves) at the corrosion potential. The linear polarization resistance (LPR) test was conducted using an input potential perturbation of ±20 mV with respect to the OCP. From this experiment, the results were obtained only in terms of polarization resistance (Rp).

To simplify complex datasets, PCA, a statistical multivariate analysis technique, was utilised wherein it transforms a large set of correlated variables into principal components, which is a smaller set of uncorrelated variables, while keeping most of the original information. It was performed using density functional theory (DFT) data obtained from a previous study [20] to validate the best, moderate and least correlations among the energy parameters (EHOMO, ELUMO and ΔE). Moreover, it was adopted to numerically prove which compounds are highly interactive and contributing to corrosion inhibition, based on the criteria obtained from experimental/theoretical DFT calculations (high EHOMO, low ELUMO and low ΔE). The analysis was conducted using parameters such as the mean, standard deviation, correlation matrix, eigenvalues and eigen vectors of the matrix.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed using a Carl Zeiss instrument on three types of samples (control, 11 ml and 29 ml) after the compressive strength test to identify cement hydration products. Powdered samples subjected to the analysis were collected from the concrete core and were gold sputter-coated prior to SEM investigation. It was performed to augment the surface conductivity and to obtain clear and high-resolution images. The working distance (WD) was maintained between 12 and 14 mm and the accelerating voltage applied to the electron beam was around 8 kV.

Pure neem extract was subjected to qualitative GC-MS investigation to directly identify the key compounds responsible for corrosion inhibition and indirectly determine the related functional groups, heteroatoms and bond type. The chromatogram results were compared with a validated reference database (NIST library) to identify the major compounds. The study was conducted adopting Agilent Technologies, in which the gas chromatograph (separation unit) used was 7890B GC and the mass selective detector (MSD) (identification unit) was 5977B MSD. Under the GC unit, a splitless type of injector was used and the volume of solution injected was 3 μL. The injector temperature for converting the liquid to gas was maintained at 280 °C. Helium was used as the carrier gas to transport the analytes from the injector to the GC column. The GC capillary column (DB-5ms) of 30 m column length, 250 μm inner diameter and 0.25 μm film thickness was considered as the stationary phase where the separation of compounds took place. The maximum temperature maintained in the oven was 325 °C.

The compressive strength results (N/mm2) of the concrete samples cured for 7 and 28 days are provided in Table 2 . Owing to the addition of a superplasticizer (0.2 % by weight of the cement), the expected target strength of around 43.25 N/mm2 was achieved at 28 days. A strength of nearly 70 % was attained at 7 days. An increase in strength was observed with increasing concentrations of neem extract. The increase in strength arising from the partial replacement of water with neem extract during concrete mixing can be ascribed to the presence of phytochemicals in neem that contribute to pore refinement. These phytochemicals refine larger capillary pores into finer pores and may promote densification of gel pores, thus increasing the density of the concrete matrix and in turn improving its strength. They are nanosized organic molecules capable of penetrating the concrete matrix and chemically interacting with the hydration products, leading to refinement and densification of gel pores. Additionally, phytochemicals comprising hydroxyl and ether functional groups display hydrophilic behaviour and can act as internal curing agents. These functional groups retain water during the mixing phase through hydrogen bonding and gradually release it during curing, especially to the unreacted cement grains within the inner matrix [22]. This curing mechanism ameliorates hydration, reduces early-age shrinkage, and contributes to strength improvement. Previous researches have reported that phytochemicals principally responsible for corrosion inhibition such as nimbolide and ellagic acid, contain both hydroxyl and ether functional groups, whereas compounds like quercetin and kaempferol possess only hydroxyl functional groups.

Fig. 2 illustrates the anodic and cathodic polarization plots of the unmodified and neem-modified RCC samples exposed to NaCl solution. The natural active corrosion phenomenon could be observed in the control sample as evidenced by a slightly more positive Ecorr than that of the inhibitor-treated samples. In the presence of neem extract, a slightly more negative shift in Ecorr values was evident, indicating reduced corrosion activity [12,17]. This is supported by the observations presented in Table 3 , where the corrosion current density (Icorr) decreased with a negative shift in Ecorr as the neem concentration increased, indicating the adsorption and protective layer formation of phytochemical compounds on the steel surface. This layer acts as a barrier, thereby slowing down both the anodic and cathodic reactions by limiting electron transfer along the steel surface. Extrapolation of anodic and cathodic curves facilitated the evaluation of corrosion potential (Ecorr), corrosion current density (icorr), Tafel slopes (βa and βc) and the corrosion rate (CR). The maximum shift in Ecorr in the presence of neem was 36 mV (<85 mV), confirming mixed adsorption behaviour [23]. From Table 3 and in the presence of neem, the variations in βa were greater than that in βc, indicating that the anodic reaction was more sensitive to changes in potential. The highest corrosion current density and corrosion rate were observed in the control sample. With the addition of neem, the corrosion rate decreased, with the lowest value observed for the 29 ml sample. Chloride ions from NaCl enter the concrete pores and accumulate at the steel–concrete interface. When the chloride concentration surpasses the critical level, the passivation layer is disrupted, resulting in active corrosion of the rebar, which is manifested by an increase in corrosion rate in the control sample. In contrast, the incorporation of neem extract reduces the corrosion current density due to the adsorption of bioactive compounds containing heteroatoms and π-electrons onto the surface of steel. This adsorption blocks the access of aggressive aqueous species, thereby suppressing both anodic and cathodic reactions through a mixed inhibition mechanism. Besides, neem used as a partial replacement in concrete, reduces the aggressiveness of chloride ions in the concrete pore solution. It is also evident from Fig. 2 that the addition of neem extract shifts both the anodic and cathodic curves to lower current values, indicating a reduction in the corrosion current density. The χ2 values obtained for the three sample types were on the order of 10−12 to 10−8, representing an excellent goodness of fit. Small variations among repeated measurements are accredited to experimental noise and surface heterogeneity. The average χ2 values obtained for the three sample types were 1.43 E−8, 3.56 E−9 and 1.33 E−9 respectively. The factors influencing the corrosion rate are: corrosion current density (icorr), equivalent weight (EW), density (ρ) and unit conversion constant (K). The equation to calculate the corrosion rate (CR) is derived from the faradays law as shown below [24],
C R =
K
i
c o r r
E W
ρ

The observations and results of the LPR test are presented in Fig. 3 and Table 3, respectively. The results are expressed solely in terms of polarization resistance and are independent of the Tafel slopes and corrosion current density. The corrosion rate was predicted based on the polarization resistance [19]. The lowest polarization resistance was obtained for the control sample, indicating free and facile electron flow through the steel and a faster release of Fe2+ ions into the solution, resulting in a higher rust volume. The results from both the Tafel and LPR plots were obtained based on the average of three samples. The percentage variation in LPR was greater than that of the Tafel results for the control, 11 ml and 29 ml samples (13.010 %, 7.32 %, 6.54 %, respectively), due to high sensitivity of polarization resistance to small potential perturbations near the corrosion potential, slight variations in surface conditions after repeated tests and error amplification during slope determination where small errors or noise in current can cause larger changes in Rp.

Fig. 4 a and b shows complex plane plots (resistance vs reactance) for specimens with and without two different concentrations of neem extract, along with the equivalent circuit model that best fits the experimental data. The corresponding resistance and capacitance values determined for both steel and concrete are listed in Table 4 . The circuit shown in Fig. 4b consists of the solution resistance (Rs), as evidenced by the presence of a non-zero value at the high-frequency intercept. It is connected in series with the material components (steel and concrete) of the circuit, in accordance with the experimental arrangement. The resistance and capacitance components of the concrete/steel system are connected in parallel, as both processes occur simultaneously at the same electrode–electrolyte interface under the same interfacial potential. Only a minor difference in the solution resistance (Rs) was observed after the addition of neem extract, signifying that the conductivity of the salt solution remained unaltered. This confirms that the main role of neem is to passivate the steel surface via bioactive phytochemical compounds. The concrete resistance increased at a concentration of 29 ml, which correlated strongly with the strength test results. This finding suggests that the phytochemical compounds acted as micro-fillers, arresting pore connectivity and densifying the concrete, thereby reducing the ionic flow movement towards the steel. The pure capacitance (Cdl) includes the stern layer (inner and outer Helmholtz planes) which is directly adjacent to the steel surface within the concrete pore solution. The double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of steel (μF) was higher than that of concrete (nF), indicating corrosion at the steel surface due to interaction with the environment and the accumulation of aggressive ions in the inner Helmholtz plane.

The higher steel resistance and lower double-layer capacitance observed in the presence of neem emerge from reduced aggressive ion movement in the Helmholtz region, owing to the protective film formation driven by heteroatoms of the bioactive compounds [2]. These electronegative atoms reduced both electron transfer from the anode to the cathode and ionic movement in the solution, leading to reduced deposits of rust over time. The samples soaked in a 1M NaCl solution, exhibited diffusion-controlled behaviour (mass transport limitations), as represented by the Warburg Impedance (W) [4,25]. The diffusion-controlled process occurs due to limited oxygen availability at the cathodic regions of the steel surface, where oxygen is usually consumed and reduced to OH− ions. This results in the formation of concentration gradients, with lower oxygen concentration near the steel surface than in the bulk solution. This form of corrosion is particularly dangerous, because it induces sudden and unpredictable failure due to uneven or localised corrosion attack often linked to deep pits. The decrease in Warburg impedance (W) with neem suggests the formation of a more uniform and stable surface with reduced rust development, implying reduced diffusion control. The presence of oxygen heteroatoms in neem reduces mass transport limitations by adsorbing onto the surface of steel, as reported in the parameters listed in Table 4. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5 , the magnitude of impedance was higher for the sample containing 29 ml neem in the low and medium frequency regions, conveying a lower corrosion rate due to effective surface passivation [26]. The mid frequency region, associated with the electrochemical double layer, is dominated by the resistance and capacitance of both concrete and steel. With the addition of neem, the resistance increased while the capacitance decreased, which is due to the replacement of aqueous chloride ions by the compounds present in neem. The low-frequency response is dominated by the overall resistance of the system, whereas, the high-frequency intercepts represent only solution resistance. As a result, the modulus of impedance is maximum at low frequencies where it is governed by the total resistance of steel and concrete. The average χ2 values obtained for the control, 11 ml and 29 ml samples were 0.0476, 0.0607 and 0.0580, respectively. Compared to DC techniques, EIS exhibited higher standard deviation and χ2 values because of higher complexity of the electrochemical system (especially within the double layer region), enhanced sensitivity to noise, indirect and complex corrosion parameters functioning differently etc.

This study was performed for three energy parameters, as shown in Fig. 6 . The theoretical DFT calculations confirmed that the compounds in the aqueous medium displayed high EHOMO, low ELUMO and low energy gap (ΔE), indicating strong chemical reactivity with respect to the surface of steel. To validate these results, PCA statistics was carried out to confirm the correlations among the energy parameters, identify the parameters giving rise to stronger interactions and, verify that the interaction trends obtained were aligned with the DFT results. The outputs, such as mean, standard deviation, correlation matrix, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, principal component numbers and their respective percentages are provided in Table 5. Analysis of the standard deviation values from Table 5 a, indicated that ΔE exhibited the highest standard deviation of 1.12987, confirming greater variability among the investigated compounds. Therefore, it is an important parameter that might influence PCA in distinguishing compound reactivity trends. In contrast, the comparatively low standard deviation of EHOMO implies that it is not a dominant factor governing PCA for distinguishing compounds in terms of their reactivity or inhibition efficiency.

Based on the correlation matrix reported in Table 5 b, a strong positive correlation of 0.98116 was evident between ELUMO and ΔE, demonstrating that a decrease in ΔE is coupled with a corresponding decrease in ELUMO. This strong correlation suggests that these parameters have the potential to play a leading role in controlling electronic reactivity. In contrast, the correlations between ELUMO vs. EHOMO and EHOMO vs. ΔE, were weak to moderately negative, signifying that a decrease in ELUMO is connected with only a slight increase in EHOMO with limited variability. PCA later uses this correlation matrix to calculate the eigenvalues and eigenvectors, as shown in Tables 5 c and 5d. The sum of the eigenvalues is equal to the number of variables = 3. A greater eigenvalue, represents a greater dominance of the corresponding component. Based on the variability and correlation data of the energy parameters, principal component (PC1) was largely influenced by ΔE and ELUMO, whereas, the principal component (PC2) was potentially influenced by EHOMO. Therefore, ΔE and ELUMO dominated the variance, confirming their controlling role in governing the reactivity trends. Using the correlation matrix and the identity matrix, the eigen values (λ1, λ2 and λ3 = 0) were calculated, as shown below in the equation [27],
det
(
R − λ I
)
= 0

The percentage variations of PC1 and PC2 were calculated from their respective eigenvalues. The eigenvectors (ν) represent the directions of maximum variance and were computed as coefficients from the correlation matrix to identify the most influential component within each principal component [28]. As presented in Table 5d, PC1 is strongly influenced by ΔE (0.66) and ELUMO (0.63), indicating that these parameters dominate PC1 meanwhile, PC2 is primarily governed by EHOMO. The loading plot from Fig. 7 additionally supports these findings, where the small angle between ΔE and ELUMO demonstrate a strong positive correlation, consistent with the Pearson correlation coefficient (R = 0.981). In contrast, the angular orientation between EHOMO and the other two parameters (ELUMO and ΔE) was nearly orthogonal, suggesting a weak and negative correlation. Thus, ΔE and ELUMO emerge as the most significant contributors to the total variance and are key descriptors for molecular reactivity based on PCA. From Table 5 e, PCA scores were calculated using the raw data of the energy parameters of each compound, along with their corresponding means, standard deviations and eigenvectors. The results from Table 5e proved that the reactivity trend of high EHOMO, low ELUMO and ΔE from PCA was analogous with those obtained from DFT calculations performed using the ORCA software.

Fig. 8 a, b and c display the SEM images of the control, 11 ml and 29 ml samplesrespectively. The hydration products, such as C-S-H gel, calcium hydroxide and ettringite, along with defects such as gel pores, were observed in Fig. 8a, which are well known to exist. Unlike Fig. 8a, the development of small fibre-like structures was noticed between the gel gaps, indicating the presence of outer C-S-H gel (looser structure). Consequently, an increased formation of C-S-H gel, along with reduced gel pore visibility, was observed, indicating improved hydration and pore refinement, as evident in Fig. 8b. The coexistence of ettringite and monosulfate phases suggests a more stable hydration environment, whereas the reduced prominence of calcium hydroxide crystals reflects their enhanced consumption during secondary hydration reactions. In the right top corner of Fig. 8b, the presence of inner C-S-H gel (denser) is noticed, which further enhances the concrete's morphology. At the highest neem concentration (Fig. 8c), distinct layered formations of C-S-H gel dominate the matrix, accompanied by minimal pore presence and tightly packed hydration products, indicating significant pore refinement and matrix densification. Although calcium hydroxide is still present, it was well embedded within the dense C-S-H network, suggesting improved interfacial bonding. Furthermore, monosulfate and ettringite phases were minimal and therefore not visible in Fig. 8c. Overall, the SEM images explicitly reveal a transition from a porous and heterogeneous matrix in the control mix to a highly compact and refined microstructure at 29 ml. This improvement in the microstructure results in enhanced concrete strength relative to its counterparts [29]. This is probably the cause for the observed strength improvement of the concrete cube at a neem concentration of 29 ml.

The volatile and semi-volatile compounds that are predominantly present in neem extract solution are depicted in the chromatogram (Fig. 9 ). Owing to their abundance (higher peak intensity and larger peak area), these compounds can possibly engage in the steel adsorption process and aid in the overall corrosion inhibition of the structure. Based on the peak height, peak area (signal intensity) and match score, the compounds present in higher abundance were identified.

The compounds present at higher relative concentrations are listed in descending order in Table 6 . The analysis was carried out for a period of 45 min, during which the major peaks were obtained at different retention times, representing nine different compounds. The identification was validated based on the peak height from Fig. 9 and the respective area values displayed in Table 6. The functional groups present in these compounds comprise esters (carbonyl and alkoxy), alkenes, carboxylic acids (carbonyl and hydroxyl), cyclic amides (amide nitrogen and carbonyl), ketones, alcohols and secondary amines. The heteroatoms (oxygen and nitrogen) present in these compounds act as the primary contributors to corrosion inhibition by serving as adsorption sites. Supporting contributors include π – electrons (from carbonyl groups, alkenes and aromatic rings), as well as molecular size, shape and geometry. Therefore, these functional groups form the chemical framework that influences inhibition effectiveness by governing the availability, orientation, stability and interaction of both the principal and supporting contributors [30].

This work primarily focused on assessing the effect of neem leaf extract on the strength and electrochemical properties of concrete at lower concentrations (11 ml and 29 ml). Some of the central reasons for analysing only these samples are as follows.
•
A lower concentration of green corrosion inhibitor is more appropriate for real-world RCC applications in terms of practicality, economy and sustainability. Although no specialised instruments or equipment were used for the extraction process (maceration), and the soaking time was fixed and predictable, multiple important factors can affect practicality when higher dosages are adopted. These encompass scalability, standardization and quality consistency. Some of the challenges arising from higher dosages may involve the demand for large immersion or soaking tanks, handling the complexity of the huge volumes of plant parts, filtration and transportation of plant extract to the required region of the construction site, batch-to-batch consistency challenges, a higher probability of human error and storage stability concerns. From an economical aspect, some of the drawbacks of using extracts at higher concentrations include increased labour charges and processing of extract, additional handling requirements, higher storage and transportation costs, and an increased risk of rework due to quality difference between different batches. From a sustainability perspective, the use of higher dosages of neem extract in large scale concrete construction can negatively influence both human health and the environment. This is due to amplified consumption of neem leaves from trees as they are widely used for many other different applications especially medicinal purposes. Furthermore, increased energy consumption, greater material wastage after filtration and higher water demand contributes further.
•
The reduction in corrosion rate observed when comparing the control sample with the 11 ml concentration (lowest) was greater than that recorded at other higher concentrations. Although the corrosion rate reduced with increasing neem extract concentration, the difference in corrosion rate reduction was minimal. Similar trends have been documented by numerous researchers investigating steel and RCC corrosion inhibition employing green corrosion inhibitors [2,31–33].
•
In the previous research [20], electrochemical tests were performed on different steels exposed to acidic HCl solution to inhibit corrosion using neem extract and other synthetic organic inhibitors. That study was aimed at corrosion control using inhibitors during cleaning processes (removal of rust and oxide scales) typically carried out in manufacturing industries and construction sites, constituting a one-time application of the plant extract. In contrast, the purpose of green corrosion inhibitors in concrete is to enhance both mechanical and durability properties over the long run.
•
A higher dosage of neem extract may also lead to increased workability [34], however overdosing give rise to bleeding and segregation issues due to the presence of some of the organic compounds such as sugars, saponins, polyphenols, organic acids, fatty acids and esters, ultimately leading to a decrease in strength and durability of the structure.

Based on the experimental results and discussions, the following conclusions were drawn:
•
The incorporation of neem extract as a partial replacement for mixing water in concrete was found to be significantly effective. The bioactive phytochemical compounds present in neem function as chemical modifiers by reducing concrete pore size and pore connectivity, thereby densifying the concrete matrix and ultimately enhancing compressive strength. The addition of neem, reduces both gel and capillary pores, with a more pronounced effect on gel pore densification at the nanoscale level. It promotes the formation of a denser C–S–H gel through chemical interaction with Ca2+ ions and hydration products, leading to refinement of the gel pore structure. Capillary pore size refinement results from improved hydration induced by the neem addition in concrete, rather than from direct chemical interaction of neem phytochemicals with capillary pores.
•
In the destructive Tafel analysis, at approximately ±50 mV away from Ecorr, increasing inhibitor concentration led to a decrease in the corrosion current density and corrosion rate, accompanied by a corresponding slight shift in corrosion potential toward negative values, implying enhanced corrosion inhibition. The lowest corrosion rate was obtained for the specimen containing 29 ml concentration of neem extract. This behaviour is ascribed to the adsorption of electronegative heteroatoms onto the surface of steel, resulting in the formation of a protective film that inhibits the ingress of aggressive Cl− ions. The concentration of chloride ions in concrete pore solution was lower due to the densification of the concrete matrix with reduced pore volume.
•
The polarization resistance calculated from LPR measurements was independent of the Tafel slopes and increased with higher inhibitor concentrations. As the icorr is inversely proportional to Rp, the corrosion rate could be indirectly predicted (icorr) and the results were well aligned with the destructive Tafel tests. The highest polarization resistance of 1647.9 Ω was recorded for the specimen incorporating 29 ml of neem extract.
•
EIS investigation, demonstrated higher resistance and lower capacitance values of steel for inhibitor containing samples, suggesting reduced electron movement, suppressed Fe++ ion release and reduced Cl− ions presence in the Helmholtz region. This performance may be ascribed to increased concrete resistance, formation of passivation layer surrounding the steel or a joint effect of both. As a result, the volume of rust development would be markedly reduced due to the suppression of both anodic and cathodic reactions, thereby facilitating a longer service life of RCC structures exposed to salt environments. Diffusion-controlled corrosion is typically linked to unpredictable failure, owing to the fact that the concentration gradients within the electrolyte can induce localized galvanic cells, consequently causing localized corrosion and deep pitting.
•
The theoretical DFT relationships were statistically validated using Principal component analysis (PCA). ΔE and ELUMO demonstrated a strong positive correction of 0.98116 compared with other parameter combinations, suggesting that these two parameters play an important role in governing the chemical reactivity of the compounds towards steel. This information was further supported by their higher eigenvalues, eigen vectors and contributions to the principal components. The compounds identified by PCA exhibiting superior corrosion inhibition based on their high PCA scores, followed a trend similar with the theoretical DFT results.
•
SEM findings confirmed that the addition of neem extract, substantially enhanced the concrete's microstructure. Densely packed layered arrangement of C-S-H gel with decreased pore presence indicate densification of concrete matrix. Interfacial bonding improved as calcium hydroxide was effectively integrated in the dense C-S-H network.
•
The major phytochemical compound in the aqueous extract of neem and their associated functional groups and heteroatoms was detected through GC-MS analysis. Approximately nine major compounds possessing oxygen and nitrogen heteroatoms were detected. These compounds are the potential agents to the corrosion inhibition of RCC.

The authors are grateful to Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore for providing the support to conduct the experimental work. However, no funding or grants was received for this research from any agencies.

E.S. Junaid Ahmed: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. G. Mohan Ganesh: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
